title: Eleanor Roosevelt
teaser: Meet the shy girl who grew up to become the longest-serving First Lady in American history, gave a voice to people no one else would listen to, and helped write a promise of rights for every person on Earth.

summary.intro: Eleanor Roosevelt (1884–1962) started life as a shy, lonely girl who lost both of her parents before she turned ten. But she grew into one of the bravest and most caring leaders America has ever known. As First Lady for twelve years — longer than anyone before or since — she completely changed what a president's wife could do: she held her own press conferences, wrote a newspaper column read by millions, and traveled the country to see how ordinary people were really living. After leaving the White House, she helped create the Universal Declaration of Human Rights at the United Nations — a promise that every person in the world deserves to be treated with dignity. President Harry Truman called her 'the First Lady of the World.'

sections.title: A Shy and Lonely Childhood
sections.body: Eleanor Roosevelt was born on October 11, 1884, in New York City, into one of America's most famous families — her uncle was Theodore Roosevelt, who would become president. But her early life was full of sadness. Her beautiful mother called her 'Granny' because she seemed so serious, and Eleanor grew up believing she was plain and awkward. When she was eight, her mother died. Less than two years later, her beloved father died too. Orphaned before age ten, Eleanor went to live with her strict grandmother, where she felt lonelier than ever. Everything changed at fifteen, when she was sent to Allenswood, a boarding school near London, England. The headmistress, Marie Souvestre, saw something special in Eleanor and taught her to think for herself, speak up, and care about people who were less fortunate. Eleanor later said those were the happiest years of her life — the years when the shy girl began to find her voice.

sections.title: Marrying Franklin
sections.body: When Eleanor returned to New York at eighteen, she did something unusual for a young woman of her wealthy world: she volunteered at a settlement house, teaching dance and exercise classes to children of poor immigrant families in New York City. Around that same time, she began spending time with a charming distant cousin named Franklin Delano Roosevelt. Franklin was amazed by how much Eleanor knew and cared about the world. They married on March 17, 1905, and Eleanor's uncle, President Theodore Roosevelt, walked her down the aisle. Over the next years Eleanor raised five children while Franklin's political career took off. Then, in 1921, disaster struck: Franklin caught polio, an illness that left his legs paralyzed. Many people thought his career was over. Eleanor refused to let that happen. She became his 'eyes, ears, and legs' — traveling, giving speeches, and keeping his name alive in politics. In helping Franklin, Eleanor discovered she was a talented leader in her own right.

sections.title: A New Kind of First Lady
sections.body: When Franklin became president in 1933, America was deep in the Great Depression — millions of people had lost their jobs and homes. First Ladies before Eleanor had mostly hosted parties and stayed quiet. Eleanor had other plans. She became the first First Lady to hold her own press conferences — and she only allowed women reporters, which forced newspapers to hire more women. She wrote a newspaper column called 'My Day' six days a week, read by millions. She traveled tens of thousands of miles every year, visiting coal mines, farms, and struggling neighborhoods, then telling Franklin exactly what Americans needed. She fought hard for fairness, too. In 1939, when an organization refused to let Marian Anderson, a great Black opera singer, perform in their concert hall because of her race, Eleanor resigned from the group in protest — and helped arrange for Anderson to sing at the Lincoln Memorial instead, before a crowd of 75,000 people.

sections.title: First Lady of the World
sections.body: Franklin died in April 1945, just as World War II was ending. Eleanor thought her public life was over — she told reporters, 'The story is over.' She was wrong. President Harry Truman asked her to become one of America's first delegates to the brand-new United Nations, an organization created to help countries solve problems with words instead of wars. There, Eleanor took on the hardest job of all: leading the committee that wrote the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. For two years she guided people from many different countries, often arguing late into the night, until they agreed on a list of rights belonging to every human being — the right to be safe, to go to school, to speak freely, to be treated equally. On December 10, 1948, the United Nations adopted the Declaration, and the delegates rose and gave Eleanor a standing ovation. It remains one of the most important documents ever written.

sections.title: Eleanor's Legacy
sections.body: Eleanor never stopped working for others. She kept writing 'My Day' nearly until her death, hosted discussions at her beloved cottage Val-Kill in Hyde Park, New York, and pushed leaders of both political parties to do more for civil rights. In 1961, President John F. Kennedy appointed her to lead the brand-new Presidential Commission on the Status of Women, studying how to make life fairer for American women. She died on November 7, 1962, at age 78, and presidents, kings, and ordinary citizens around the world mourned her. Today, Eleanor is remembered as proof that you don't have to be born brave — you can become brave by doing the things you fear. Her famous advice still inspires people everywhere: 'You must do the thing you think you cannot do.' Her home at Val-Kill is now a National Historic Site, the only one dedicated to a First Lady.

keyFacts: Eleanor Roosevelt was First Lady for twelve years (1933–1945) — longer than anyone else in American history.
She was the first First Lady to hold her own press conferences, and she allowed only women reporters so newspapers would have to hire women.

In 1939 she resigned from an organization that refused to let singer Marian Anderson perform because she was Black — then helped arrange Anderson's famous concert at the Lincoln Memorial for 75,000 people.
She led the United Nations committee that wrote the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, adopted on December 10, 1948.
President Harry Truman called her 'the First Lady of the World' for her work helping people everywhere.

videos.title: Who Was Eleanor Roosevelt? | All About Eleanor Roosevelt for Kids | First Lady | Twinkl USA
videos.description: Twinkl USA's classroom-friendly overview of Eleanor Roosevelt's life, covering her record four terms as First Lady from 1933 and how she changed the role forever — a solid first introduction to the topic.

videos.title: Eleanor Roosevelt Fun Facts for Kids
videos.description: A fun, fast-moving biography for kids highlighting the most memorable facts about Eleanor Roosevelt — great as a quick refresher before taking the quiz.

videos.title: The Life of Eleanor Roosevelt for Kids
videos.description: A narrated walk through Eleanor Roosevelt's whole life — from lonely orphan to First Lady of the World — focused on how she helped people and made a difference, pairing well with the Learn More chapters.

readings.title: Eleanor Roosevelt: A Life of Discovery
readings.author: Russell Freedman
readings.citationChicago: Freedman, Russell. 1993. Eleanor Roosevelt: A Life of Discovery. New York: Clarion Books.
readings.summary: This Newbery Honor–winning biography is one of the best children's books ever written about a First Lady. Russell Freedman tells Eleanor's whole story — the lonely childhood, the years beside Franklin, the battles for fairness, and the United Nations triumph — alongside more than one hundred real black-and-white photographs from her life. Freedman never talks down to readers: he shows Eleanor as a real person who was often afraid but acted anyway. Best for confident readers ages 10 and up, or as a read-together book for younger kids who love the photos.

readings.title: Who Was Eleanor Roosevelt?
readings.author: Gare Thompson
readings.citationChicago: Thompson, Gare. 2004. Who Was Eleanor Roosevelt? Illustrated by Elizabeth Wolf. New York: Grosset & Dunlap.
readings.summary: Part of the popular Who Was? chapter-book series, this friendly biography follows Eleanor from a shy child who had little confidence in herself to a First Lady who refused to be just a hostess. Gare Thompson shows how Eleanor used her voice to speak up against poverty and racism, with black-and-white illustrations on nearly every page. Written especially for readers ages 8–12, it is the perfect first chapter book on Eleanor — easy to read on your own, but full of real history.

readings.title: Eleanor Roosevelt (FDR Presidential Library & Museum)
readings.author: Franklin D. Roosevelt Presidential Library and Museum
readings.citationChicago: Franklin D. Roosevelt Presidential Library and Museum. "Eleanor Roosevelt." FDRLibrary.org. https://www.fdrlibrary.org/eleanor-roosevelt.
readings.summary: The official website of the Franklin D. Roosevelt Presidential Library tells Eleanor's story using the real letters, photographs, and documents she left behind. This is where historians go to study the Roosevelts — and the Eleanor pages are written so students can use them too. You can see what her handwriting looked like, read about her travels, and explore the same archives that hold her famous 'My Day' columns. Best for ages 10 and up doing reports, or for younger kids exploring with a parent.

learnMoreImages.caption: Eleanor at about age seven with her father Elliott and her brothers Elliott Jr. and Gracie Hall in New York, 1892. Born on October 11, 1884, into one of America's most famous families, Eleanor still grew up feeling plain, serious, and lonely — her own mother nicknamed her 'Granny.'

Eleanor with her beloved father Elliott Roosevelt in 1889, when she was about five years old. Her mother died when Eleanor was eight, and Elliott died less than two years later — leaving the orphaned girl to be raised by her strict grandmother.

Eleanor's school portrait from 1898, around age fourteen — just before she sailed to England to attend Allenswood. The solemn girl in this photo was about to meet headmistress Marie Souvestre, who would teach her to think for herself and speak with confidence.

President Theodore Roosevelt photographed by the Pach Brothers studio, 1904. Eleanor's father Elliott was Theodore's younger brother, which made the 26th President of the United States her uncle — and made little Eleanor part of America's most talked-about family.

Marie Souvestre, the French headmistress of Allenswood boarding school near London. Souvestre saw something special in shy Eleanor and made her a favorite pupil — Eleanor later said her years at Allenswood were the happiest of her whole life.

questions.prompt: Where was Eleanor Roosevelt born?
choices: Boston, Massachusetts
New York City, New York
Washington, D.C.
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
questions.explanation: Eleanor Roosevelt was born on October 11, 1884, in New York City, into one of America's wealthiest and most famous families — but even with all that, her childhood was lonely and full of sadness.

questions.prompt: Why did young Eleanor go to live with her strict grandmother?
choices: Her parents moved to Europe for work
Both of her parents died before she turned ten
Her grandmother needed help around the house
Her family lost all of their money
questions.correctAnswer: Both of her parents died before she turned ten
questions.explanation: Eleanor's mother died when she was eight, and her beloved father died less than two years later. Orphaned before age ten, Eleanor went to live with her strict grandmother, where she often felt lonelier than ever.

questions.prompt: How did the Allenswood school in England change Eleanor's life?
choices: She learned to play several musical instruments there
She decided to become a doctor after studying science there
Her headmistress taught her to think for herself and speak up
She met Franklin Roosevelt in one of her classes there
questions.correctAnswer: Her headmistress taught her to think for herself and speak up
questions.explanation: At fifteen, Eleanor was sent to Allenswood, a boarding school near London. The headmistress, Marie Souvestre, saw something special in the shy girl and taught her to think for herself, speak with confidence, and care about people less fortunate. Eleanor called those the happiest years of her life.

questions.prompt: Eleanor Roosevelt's uncle was Theodore Roosevelt, who became President of the United States.
questions.explanation: True. Eleanor's father, Elliott, was the younger brother of Theodore Roosevelt, who became the 26th President of the United States in 1901. Being a Roosevelt put Eleanor in one of America's most famous families from the day she was born.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the boarding school in England where shy Eleanor finally found her voice?
questions.explanation: Eleanor attended Allenswood, a boarding school near London, England, from age fifteen. Under headmistress Marie Souvestre, the shy, lonely girl blossomed into a confident young woman — she later said her years there were the happiest of her life.

learnMoreImages.caption: Eleanor in her wedding dress in New York City, 1905, from the National Archives. She married Franklin on March 17, 1905, and her uncle, President Theodore Roosevelt, walked her down the aisle in place of her late father.

One of the very few photographs of Franklin Roosevelt in his wheelchair, taken at Hyde Park in February 1941 with his dog Fala and a young friend. Polio paralyzed Franklin's legs in 1921 — the moment many people wrongly believed would end his career.

Franklin and Eleanor with Democratic Party workers in Albany, New York, during his years as governor. Franklin's comeback was possible because Eleanor had served as his 'eyes, ears, and legs' — traveling, speaking, and keeping his name alive in politics while he recovered.

An informal snapshot of newlyweds Franklin and Eleanor in Newburgh, New York, May 1905, from the National Archives. The young couple were distant cousins from two branches of the big Roosevelt family — so Eleanor never even had to change her last name.

The Rivington Street Settlement house in New York City, pictured in The Survey magazine, 1914. At eighteen, Eleanor volunteered at this settlement on the Lower East Side, teaching dance and exercise classes to children from poor immigrant families.

questions.prompt: Who walked Eleanor down the aisle at her wedding to Franklin in 1905?
choices: Her grandfather
Her older brother
Her uncle, President Theodore Roosevelt
Her headmistress, Marie Souvestre
questions.explanation: Eleanor married Franklin Delano Roosevelt on March 17, 1905, and because her father had died, her uncle — President Theodore Roosevelt himself — walked her down the aisle. The president joked that he liked to be the center of attention at every event, even someone else's wedding.

questions.prompt: What illness struck Franklin in 1921 and left his legs paralyzed?
choices: Tuberculosis
Polio
Scarlet fever
Pneumonia
questions.explanation: In 1921, Franklin caught polio, a serious illness that left his legs paralyzed for the rest of his life. Many people believed his political career was finished — but Eleanor refused to let that happen.

questions.prompt: How did Eleanor help Franklin's political career after his illness?
choices: She took over his job as governor of New York
She became his 'eyes, ears, and legs,' traveling and speaking for him
She hired doctors to cure his paralysis completely
She wrote his speeches but never appeared in public
questions.correctAnswer: She became his 'eyes, ears, and legs,' traveling and speaking for him
questions.explanation: After polio paralyzed Franklin's legs, Eleanor became his 'eyes, ears, and legs' — traveling around the country, giving speeches, and keeping his name alive in politics. In helping Franklin, Eleanor discovered she was a talented leader in her own right.

questions.prompt: Eleanor and Franklin Roosevelt were distant cousins who shared the same last name before they married.
questions.explanation: True. Eleanor and Franklin were distant cousins from two branches of the large Roosevelt family, so Eleanor was born a Roosevelt and stayed a Roosevelt when she married. Franklin admired how much she knew and cared about the world around her.

questions.prompt: Before she married Franklin, what did Eleanor do to help children from poor immigrant families in New York City?
questions.correctAnswer: She taught classes at a settlement house
questions.explanation: At eighteen, Eleanor volunteered at a settlement house in New York City, teaching dance and exercise classes to children from poor immigrant families. It was unusual work for a wealthy young woman — and it opened her eyes to how hard life was for many Americans.

aspects.name: A New Kind of First Lady

learnMoreImages.caption: Eleanor speaking into a bank of microphones at the Century of Progress World's Fair in Chicago, November 1933, from the FDR Presidential Library. Through speeches, radio, and her six-day-a-week 'My Day' column, she talked directly to millions of Americans like no First Lady before her.

Eleanor's portrait from 1933, the year she moved into the White House — and the year she held the first press conference ever given by a First Lady. By inviting only women reporters, she made newspapers across the country hire women journalists.

Marian Anderson singing at the Lincoln Memorial on Easter Sunday, April 9, 1939, before a crowd of 75,000, from the National Archives. Eleanor's protest resignation helped turn an act of unfairness into one of the most famous concerts in American history.

Franklin and Eleanor in Washington in 1933, at the very start of their White House years. Franklin was elected president four times, making Eleanor First Lady for twelve years — from 1933 to 1945, longer than anyone else in American history.

Eleanor visiting a National Youth Administration project, where young people found work and training during the Great Depression. She traveled tens of thousands of miles a year to see how Americans were really living — then told Franklin exactly what they needed.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the newspaper column Eleanor wrote six days a week?
choices: 'Dear America'
'My Day'
'The First Lady Speaks'
'Letters from the White House'
questions.explanation: Eleanor wrote a newspaper column called 'My Day' six days a week, sharing her travels, thoughts, and the problems of everyday Americans. Millions of people read it — no First Lady had ever spoken so directly to the public before.

questions.prompt: Who was allowed to attend Eleanor's press conferences at the White House?
choices: Only radio announcers
Only reporters from New York newspapers
Only women reporters
Only photographers
questions.explanation: Eleanor was the first First Lady ever to hold her own press conferences — and she allowed only women reporters to attend. That clever rule forced newspapers across the country to hire women journalists if they wanted the news from the White House.

questions.prompt: What did Eleanor do in 1939 when an organization refused to let singer Marian Anderson perform because she was Black?
choices: She wrote a private letter asking them to reconsider
She resigned from the group and helped arrange a concert at the Lincoln Memorial
She asked Franklin to close down the organization
She bought the concert hall so Anderson could sing there
questions.correctAnswer: She resigned from the group and helped arrange a concert at the Lincoln Memorial
questions.explanation: When an organization refused to let the great opera singer Marian Anderson perform in their hall because of her race, Eleanor publicly resigned from the group in protest — and helped arrange for Anderson to sing at the Lincoln Memorial instead, before a crowd of 75,000 people.

questions.prompt: Eleanor Roosevelt was First Lady for four years, the same as most First Ladies.
questions.explanation: False. Eleanor was First Lady for twelve years, from 1933 to 1945 — longer than anyone else in American history. Franklin was elected president four times, and Eleanor used every one of those years to travel, write, and speak up for ordinary Americans.

questions.prompt: What hard time was America going through when Franklin became president in 1933, with millions of people out of work?
questions.explanation: When Franklin became president in 1933, America was deep in the Great Depression — millions of people had lost their jobs and homes. Eleanor traveled tens of thousands of miles a year to see how people were really living, then told Franklin exactly what Americans needed.

learnMoreImages.caption: Eleanor at a United Nations meeting in London, England, from the National Archives. President Truman appointed her one of America's first UN delegates, and she traveled to London for the very first meeting of the General Assembly in January 1946.

Eleanor at a Human Rights Commission meeting at Lake Success, New York, from the National Archives. As chair of this commission, she led the long, difficult work of writing the Universal Declaration of Human Rights — a list of rights for every person on Earth.

Eleanor proudly holding a poster of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, photographed in 1949, from the FDR Presidential Library. The United Nations adopted the Declaration on December 10, 1948 — and the delegates rose to give Eleanor a standing ovation.

Eleanor holding the Universal Declaration of Human Rights printed in Spanish, from the FDR Presidential Library. Because she guided delegates from many countries to agreement, the Declaration belonged to the whole world — and has been translated into more languages than any other document.
Eleanor at the United Nations in 1947, where she became one of the most respected voices in the world. President Truman gave her the nickname that stuck forever: 'the First Lady of the World.'

questions.prompt: What new organization did President Truman ask Eleanor to join as one of America's first delegates?
choices: The Red Cross
The United Nations
The Peace Corps
The Supreme Court
questions.explanation: After Franklin died in 1945, President Harry Truman asked Eleanor to become one of America's first delegates to the brand-new United Nations — an organization created to help countries solve problems with words instead of wars.

questions.prompt: What famous document did the committee Eleanor led create at the United Nations?
choices: The Declaration of Independence
The Treaty of Versailles
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights
The United States Constitution
questions.correctAnswer: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights
questions.explanation: Eleanor led the United Nations committee that wrote the Universal Declaration of Human Rights — a list of rights belonging to every person on Earth, like the right to be safe, to go to school, to speak freely, and to be treated equally.

questions.prompt: What happened on December 10, 1948, after two years of Eleanor's hard work at the United Nations?
choices: Eleanor was elected president of the United Nations
The United Nations adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights
Eleanor won the Nobel Peace Prize
The United Nations moved its headquarters to Washington, D.C.
questions.correctAnswer: The United Nations adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights
questions.explanation: On December 10, 1948, the United Nations adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights — and the delegates rose to give Eleanor a standing ovation. It remains one of the most important documents ever written, translated into more languages than any other document in the world.

questions.prompt: Eleanor wrote the Universal Declaration of Human Rights all by herself, without help from anyone else.
questions.explanation: False. Eleanor led a committee of people from many different countries with very different ideas. Her great skill was guiding them — often through arguments that lasted late into the night — until they could all agree on rights that belong to every human being.

questions.prompt: What nickname did President Truman give Eleanor because of her work helping people everywhere?
questions.explanation: President Harry Truman called Eleanor 'the First Lady of the World' because she no longer worked just for Americans — through the United Nations and her travels, she worked for the rights and dignity of people everywhere on Earth.

learnMoreImages.caption: Eleanor with President John F. Kennedy during a visit at her Val-Kill home in the early 1960s. Kennedy asked the 77-year-old Eleanor to lead his new Presidential Commission on the Status of Women — her final great public job.

Eleanor relaxed and smiling at Val-Kill in Hyde Park, from the National Archives. The confident woman in this photo had once been a frightened, lonely girl — proof of her own famous advice that you become brave by doing the thing you think you cannot do.

Eleanor hosting boys from the Wiltwyck School at a picnic at Val-Kill in Hyde Park, New York, from the National Archives. Her beloved cottage — where she welcomed everyone from world leaders to neighborhood kids — is now the only National Historic Site dedicated to a First Lady.

Eleanor walking Franklin's dog Fala at Hyde Park after the president's death in 1945. Far from retiring, she kept writing her 'My Day' column for nearly seventeen more years — using it to speak up for civil rights, women's rights, and human rights almost until her death.

Presidents and future presidents — Harry Truman, Lyndon Johnson, and Dwight Eisenhower — at Eleanor's funeral in Hyde Park, November 1962, from the National Archives. When she died on November 7, 1962, leaders and ordinary people around the whole world mourned her.

questions.prompt: Which president asked Eleanor to lead the new Presidential Commission on the Status of Women in 1961?
choices: Harry Truman
Dwight Eisenhower
John F. Kennedy
Lyndon Johnson
questions.explanation: In 1961, President John F. Kennedy appointed Eleanor — then 77 years old — to lead the brand-new Presidential Commission on the Status of Women, which studied how to make life fairer for American women in jobs, schools, and the law.

questions.prompt: What did Eleanor mean by her famous advice, 'You must do the thing you think you cannot do'?
choices: You should only attempt things you are already good at
You become brave by facing the things that scare you
You should always ask someone else to do hard things for you
You must finish every task in a single day
questions.correctAnswer: You become brave by facing the things that scare you
questions.explanation: Eleanor believed nobody is born brave — you become brave by doing the things you fear. She had been a shy, frightened girl herself, and every speech, every trip, and every fight for fairness was her doing 'the thing she thought she could not do.'

questions.prompt: Why is Eleanor's home at Val-Kill in Hyde Park, New York, special today?
choices: It is the tallest house in New York State
It is a National Historic Site — the only one dedicated to a First Lady
It was turned into the headquarters of the United Nations
It is where the Universal Declaration of Human Rights is kept
questions.correctAnswer: It is a National Historic Site — the only one dedicated to a First Lady
questions.explanation: Eleanor's beloved cottage Val-Kill in Hyde Park, New York, is now a National Historic Site — the only one in America dedicated to a First Lady. It was the one home that was truly her own, where she hosted everyone from world leaders to neighborhood kids.

questions.prompt: Eleanor stopped writing her 'My Day' newspaper column as soon as she left the White House in 1945.
questions.explanation: False. Eleanor kept writing 'My Day' for nearly seventeen more years after leaving the White House, continuing almost until her death in 1962. Writing was how she kept speaking up for civil rights, women's rights, and human rights around the world.

questions.prompt: In what year did Eleanor Roosevelt die, mourned by presidents, kings, and ordinary people around the world?
questions.explanation: Eleanor Roosevelt died on November 7, 1962, at age 78. Leaders and ordinary citizens around the world mourned her — the shy girl who became the First Lady of the World had spent her whole life proving that one caring person really can change things.
